question: This area chart shows average housing prices ($1000s) across different cities. What makes this visualization misleading?

options.A: The chart shows a generally increasing or decreasing pattern across the categories, indicating an underlying continuous progression.
options.B: San Francisco clearly has the highest price among all cities shown.
options.C: The chart incorrectly applies a continuous filled area over unrelated city categories, creating a false impression of a smooth trend between them.
options.D: Boston clearly has the lowest price among all cities shown.

explanation: This area chart joins category values for city into a continuous filled region. However, the x‑axis categories (e.g., Seattle, Boston, Atlanta, Portland, ...) have no inherent numeric or spatial continuity, so the apparent smooth trend is an artifact of the chart type rather than a meaningful pattern. A bar chart would allow readers to compare each category independently without implying a continuous progression.
